10: exousian echei ho huios tou anthropou aphienai hamartias epi tes ges — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the first Danielic Son of Man claim in Mark (cf. Dan 7:13-14); the universal dominion given to the Son of Man in Daniel is now being exercised in the specific act of pronouncing forgiveness in a Capernaum house. Epi tes ges (on earth) is emphatic: this divine prerogative is being exercised in the present earthly ministry, not deferred to the final judgment. The visible healing is the credential of the invisible forgiveness.
19: mhe dynastai hoi huioi tou nymphonos en ho ho nymphios estin autois nesteuen — the wedding guests cannot fast while the bridegroom is with them — huioi tou nymphonos (sons of the wedding chamber) is a Semitic idiom for wedding guests; the idiom places Jesus in the role of the messianic bridegroom (cf. Isa 54:5-6, Hos 2:19). The presence of the bridegroom defines the appropriate response: feasting, not fasting. The eschatological joy is already present in Jesus's person; mourning practices are inappropriate in the kingdom's inaugural moment.
1: Kai eiselthOn palin eis Kapharnaoum di' hemErOn — and having entered again into Capernaum after some days — di' hemErOn (through days, after an interval of days) signals resumption of Capernaum-based ministry after the regional circuit of 1:39-45. Ekousthē hoti en oikO estin — it was heard that he was at home — en oikO (at home, in a house) may mean Simon's house (1:29-31) or a dwelling Jesus used regularly in Capernaum; the definite article's absence leaves both open. The spread of the report (ekousthE = it was heard, passive) without explicit announcement is consistent with the reputation-spreading dynamic of 1:28.
2: Kai synagEchthEsan polloi hOste mEketi chOrein mEde ta pros ten thyran — and many gathered so that there was no longer room even at the door — the spatial overflow repeats the pattern of 1:33 (the whole city at the door). Kai elalei autois ton logon — and he was speaking the word to them — ton logon (the word) without specification: early Christian usage of logos as a technical term for the gospel message is operative. Mark's narrative compresses the crowd scene into one clause and moves immediately to the rooftop drama — the preaching is background to the healing.
3: Kai erchontai pherein pros auton paralytikon airomenon hypo tessarOn — and they came bringing to him a paralyzed man carried by four — paralytikos (from paraluO = to loosen beside, to unloose the motor faculty) is the clinical term for motor incapacity. The specification of four carriers (hypo tessarOn) is precise and picturesque: the roof operation requires four people at the corners of the mat. The verb pherO (to carry, bear — also to bring an offering) creates the image of an offering of need being presented to the healer.
4: Kai mE dynamis prosengisai autO dia ton ochlon apestegasan ten stegen hopou En — and not being able to bring him near because of the crowd, they unroofed the roof where he was — apestegasan ten stegen (they unroofed the roof) is the NT hapax verb apostegazO. A Palestinian peasant roof was typically packed earth on wooden beams or interlaid branches, accessible by an outside staircase. Opening it required deliberate demolition: removing material to create a hole large enough for the mat. Exoruxantes chalaOsin ton krabbaton — having dug through they lowered the mat — krabbatos (pallet, cot — low-register word for a simple sleeping mat) marks the social status of the man being lowered.
5: IdOn de ho Iesous ten pistin autOn — seeing their faith — the object of idOn (having seen) is ten pistin autOn (their faith): what Jesus sees is the visible, enacted faith of those who demolished a roof. Faith in Mark is demonstrated through action rather than verbal confession; the observable expression of faith (the physical effort) is what Jesus perceives. Teknon, aphiontai sou hai hamartiai — child, your sins are being forgiven — teknon (child, term of affection) is the intimate pastoral address. Aphiontai (present passive: are being forgiven, are being released) — the forgiveness is active in the present moment of speech, not a future promise.
6: Esan de tines tOn grammateOn ekei kathEmenoi kai dialogizomenoi en tais kardiais autOn — but there were some of the scribes sitting there and deliberating in their hearts — dialogizomai (to reason through, deliberate — dia + logos) carries the sense of working through a problem argument by argument. The scribes reason silently — en tais kardiais autOn (in their hearts), the seat of thought in Hebrew-Jewish anthropology. Their internal deliberation is private until Jesus reads it (v.8).
7: Ti houtos houtOs lalei? Blasphemei — why does this one speak thus? He blasphemes — blaspheO (to harm/injure the divine reputation) was punishable by death under Lev 24:16. The scribes' theological reasoning is internally coherent: tis dynatai aphienai hamartias ei me heis ho theos — who can forgive sins except one, God? The premise is correct (forgiveness is God's prerogative); the conclusion (therefore Jesus is blaspheming) fails only if Jesus is who Mark has already announced him to be (Son of God, 1:1). The passage's Christological argument turns precisely on that identification.
8: Kai euthys epignous ho Iesous tO pneumati autou hoti houtOs dialogizontai en heautois — and immediately, knowing by his spirit that thus they were deliberating within themselves — epiginOskO (to know fully/completely) in the aorist participle: immediate and complete perception. TO pneumati autou (by/in his spirit) — direct spiritual knowledge, not deductive inference. In OT theology, knowledge of the heart's contents belongs to God alone (Jer 17:9-10; Ps 139:1-4; 1 Sam 16:7). Jesus's perception of unspoken thoughts enacts a divine prerogative, making the verse a Christological argument embedded in the healing narrative.
9: Ti estin eukopOteron, eipein tO paralytikO, Aphiontai sou hai hamartiai, E eipein, Egeire kai aron ton krabbaton sou kai peripatei — which is easier, to say to the paralyzed man "your sins are being forgiven," or to say "rise and take your mat and walk"? — the rhetorical comparison (eukopOteron = easier) is a standard argument from the greater to the lesser. Pronouncing forgiveness is verbally unverifiable by human observers; commanding a paralyzed man to rise is immediately and publicly verifiable. If the verifiable thing is accomplished, the unverifiable claim is thereby vindicated. The question does not deny the charges but reframes them on verifiable grounds.
11: Soi legO, egeire aron ton krabbaton sou kai hypage eis ton oikon sou — I say to you, rise, take your mat and go to your house — the three imperatives address the paralyzed man directly: egeire (rise — the resurrection verb as a command before the restoration), aron (take up), hypage (go). The command asserts motor function before it has been demonstrated. The reversal encoded in aron ton krabbaton (carry the mat) is pointed: the man carried on his mat will carry the mat. He who was dependent will now bear his own means of dependence as a trophy of the healing.
12: Kai Egerthē kai euthys aras ton krabbaton exElthen emprosthen pantOn — and he was raised up and immediately, having taken up the mat, he went out before all — egerthE (was raised up, passive — the resurrection passive, the same form used at 16:6 for the resurrection of Jesus). The three actions mirror the three commands of v.11: he rose, he took the mat, he went. Oidamen hoti houtOs oudepote eidamen — we have never seen it thus — the historical "never before" marks the unprecedented character of what has occurred. The crowd's amazement (existasthai = to stand outside oneself, to be beside oneself with astonishment) is the collective witness to the Christological claim enacted in the healing.
13: Kai exElthen palin para ten thalassan — and he went out again alongside the sea — the spatial rhythm of Mark's narrative: crowded house → open lakeshore. Pas ho ochlos Ercheto pros auton, kai edidasken autous — all the crowd came to him and he was teaching them — the imperfect edidasken indicates sustained teaching. The lakeside setting (open, informal, accessible from any direction) contrasts with the synagogue (structured, institutional, weekly). Jesus uses both venues: the teaching authority of v.22 operates in both settings.
14: Leuin ton tou Alphaiou kathEmenon epi to telOnion — Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax booth — the tax/toll collector (telOnEs) at his station (telOnion, tax-booth, customs post) was typically positioned at a road, bridge, or city gate to levy tolls on goods. The identification "of Alphaeus" (tou Alphaiou) appears also at 3:18 for James son of Alphaeus, raising the question of fraternal relationship and identification with Matthew (Matt 9:9; 10:3). The call pattern is the same as 1:17-20: Jesus passing, seeing, two-word call, immediate compliance.
15: Polloi telOnai kai hamartOloi synanekeinto tO IEsou kai tois mathEtais autou — many tax collectors and sinners were reclining at table with Jesus and his disciples — synanakeimai (to recline with at table) is the technical vocabulary for table fellowship in the Greco-Roman meal posture. Eating with someone in antiquity declared social solidarity and, for Jews, implied ritual compatibility. HamartOloi (sinners) here likely designates those who did not observe Torah in the Pharisaic manner — particularly regarding tithing, purity, and sabbath — rather than generically immoral persons.
16: Hoi grammateis tOn PharisaiOi idontes hoti esthiei meta tOn hamartOlOn kai telOnOn elegon tois mathEtais autou — the scribes of the Pharisees seeing that he eats with sinners and tax collectors said to his disciples — "scribes of the Pharisees" is more specific than "scribes": Torah-scholars operating within the Pharisaic purity program. The challenge is directed at the disciples (tois mathEtais autou) rather than at Jesus — a rhetorical strategy to undermine the teacher through his students. The question is indirect: "why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners?" addressed to those who should be enforcing proper standards on their teacher.
17: Ou chreian echousin hoi ischyontes iatrou all' hoi kakOs echontes — those who are strong do not need a physician but those who are sick — the medical analogy is self-evident and irrefutable: physicians go where illness is. The force of the analogy reframes the table-fellowship charge: if he associates with sinners, it is because sinners are his practice, not because he endorses their condition. Ouk Elthon kalesai dikaious alla hamartOlous — I did not come to call the righteous but sinners — kalesai (to call) picks up the discipleship-call language of the chapter: the tax collector was "called" (v.14), and the mission is to call those who are sinners. The possible irony: those who consider themselves dikaious (righteous) have no perceived need of the physician and thus place themselves outside his reach.
18: Kai Esan hoi mathEtai IOannou kai hoi PharisaiOi nEsteuontes — and the disciples of John and the Pharisees were fasting — the combination of John's disciples and the Pharisees as co-fasting groups is notable: they are theologically distinct communities aligned on this specific practice. The fasting at issue is voluntary piety-fasting (Pharisaic practice: Monday and Thursday), not the Yom Kippur fast required by Torah. The question comes from unnamed questioners (erchontai kai legousin = they come and say), creating an open challenge rather than a named-group confrontation.
20: Eleusontai de hEmerai hotan aparthE ap' autOn ho nymphios, kai tote nEsteusousin en ekeinE tE hEmera — but days will come when the bridegroom will be taken away from them, and then they will fast on that day — aparthE (aorist passive subjunctive of apairO, to take away, lift off by force) is the first unambiguous passion prediction in Mark: the bridegroom will be taken away. The passive voice indicates violent removal rather than voluntary departure. Tote nEsteusousin (then they will fast) — fasting is reinterpreted as the practice of mourning and eschatological waiting appropriate to the period of the bridegroom's absence after his death, not appropriate to the presence-period of his earthly ministry.
21: Epiblēma rhakous agnaphou epirraptei epi himation palaion — no one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment — agnaphos (unfulled, unshrunk — a- privative + gnaptO, to card or full cloth) is cloth that has not undergone the shrinking process; sewn to a worn garment it will pull away when washed, making the tear worse. Palaion (old, worn out — from palaioo, to make obsolete) is the key term for the old order. The metaphor establishes that the new content of the kingdom is qualitatively incompatible with the old wineskin/garment structures — not that the old was bad, but that the new cannot be contained within it.
22: Kai oudeis ballei oinon neon eis askous palaiOus — no one pours new wine into old wineskins — the second metaphor. The wine is neon (recently made, young, still fermenting) and the skins must be new to expand with it; old skins have lost their elasticity. Note the vocabulary distinction: v.21 uses agnaphos/kainos (new in kind); v.22 uses neos (new in time). Kainos (qualitatively new) and neos (recently made) are distinct: the kingdom is both qualitatively new (unprecedented in kind) and freshly inaugurated (recently arrived). Both dimensions require new structures — not patchwork accommodations within the existing system.
23: Kai egeneto auton en tois sabbasin paraporeuesthai dia tOn sporImOn — and it happened that on the Sabbath he was passing through the grain fields — paraporeuesthai (to travel alongside, pass through) is movement through fields, not active harvesting. The disciples' action (Erxanto hodon poiein tillontes tous stachyas = began to make a way plucking heads of grain) was permitted for hungry travelers by Deut 23:25, but the Pharisees classify it as reaping under the Sabbath restrictions. The controversy is not about Torah violation per se but about Pharisaic halakhah that expanded the Sabbath reaping-prohibition to cover gleaning. Jesus's counterargument operates on the level of hermeneutical principle, not merely exception.
24: Ide, ti poiousin tois sabbasin ho ouk exestin? — see, why do they do on the Sabbath what is not lawful? — exestin (it is lawful/permitted) is the halakhic permissibility verb. The Pharisees address Jesus rather than the disciples — the teacher is responsible for students' behavior. The challenge is not to the disciples' hunger but to Jesus's halakhic teaching: he is being charged with licensing unlawful behavior. Jesus responds not by arguing the exception but by challenging the premises: David overrode a more serious prohibition; the Sabbath was made for humanity; the Son of Man is its Lord.
25: Oudepote anegnOte ho epoiEsen Dauid, hote chreian eschen — have you never read what David did when he was in need — the appeal to scripture (ouk anegnOte = have you not read?) is Jesus's standard debate-opening (also 10:9; 12:10,26). The David precedent (1 Sam 21:1-6) establishes that chreian eschen (pressing need) overrode the purity restriction on the bread of the Presence. The argument is from precedent: if the anointed king's need overrode a specific ritual prohibition, the disciples' hunger can override a secondary Sabbath restriction derived from Torah by Pharisaic extension.
26: Epi Abiathar archiereos — in the days of Abiathar the high priest — the historical crux: 1 Sam 21:1-6 places the incident under Ahimelech, not Abiathar. Abiathar was Ahimelech's son, who became the prominent high priest of David's reign (2 Sam 15:35; 1 Kgs 2:26-27). Proposed explanations: (1) an early traditional slip, preserved by Mark; (2) the phrase means "in the section about Abiathar the high priest" (the more famous of the two, used to identify the scriptural passage); (3) the two priests may have shared the office temporarily. No fully satisfying resolution exists; the phrase is the most-discussed historical difficulty in Mark. Tous artous tEs prothEseOs (the bread of Presentation/Setting-Out) is the showbread of Lev 24:5-9, restricted to Aaronic priests.
27: To sabbaton dia ton anthropon egeneto kai ouch ho anthrOpos dia to sabbaton — the Sabbath came into being for the sake of humanity and not humanity for the sake of the Sabbath — this verse is unique to Mark; neither Matthew nor Luke includes it, moving directly to v.28. Dia ton anthropon (for the sake of humanity/the human being) is the teleological statement: the Sabbath was created as a gift and provision (Gen 2:1-3; Exod 20:8-11; Deut 5:12-15), not a burdensome institution. The principle reverses the Pharisaic trajectory: they had made human beings serve the Sabbath-institution; Jesus restores the Sabbath to its intended direction of service toward humanity.
28: HOste kyrios estin ho huios tou anthrOpou kai tou sabbatou — so the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath — the argument's climax: kyrios (lord, master, owner) of the Sabbath belongs to the Son of Man. The kai (even) is emphatic: of the Sabbath itself, the institution that God established in creation. The Son of Man language carries the Dan 7:13-14 resonances of the one given universal authority and dominion. The claim moves from humanitarian argument (v.27: Sabbath for humanity) to Christological claim (v.28: the Son of Man is its Lord), making the final word of the chapter a direct assertion of Messianic authority over the Sabbath's meaning.
